behaviors.description: The clause extractor identifies all key terms (parties, dates, obligations, penalties) present in the contract.
behaviors.justification: The clause extractor is an AI model whose accuracy depends on real LLM behavior. Mocking the extractor would hide whether it actually identifies the specified term types correctly. Real contracts with diverse clause structures and varied phrasing may expose missing or incorrect term identification that mocks won't reveal. Testing requires calling the real extractor on realistic contract excerpts to verify it captures all required term categories.
behaviors.test_description: Deploy a real clause extractor and run it on a set of diverse contract excerpts containing each required term type (party names, effective dates, term lengths, obligation descriptions, penalty clauses). Verify that the extractor identifies at least one instance of each term type when present in the contract text. Test both standard and complex phrasing. Verify that the extractor does not extract terms that aren't actually present (false positives). Run on contracts from different jurisdictions and industries to test generalization.

behaviors.description: The risk assessor flags language that would be problematic under the configured risk policy, even when the problematic phrasing is indirect or euphemistic.
behaviors.justification: The risk assessor is an AI model that must recognize problematic language including euphemisms, indirect phrasing, and implied risks. Mocking would always pass if configured to return risk flags for test inputs, hiding whether the real model actually detects subtle risks according to the policy. Testing requires calling the real risk assessor with contract language that uses different risk expressions to verify it correctly identifies risks.
behaviors.test_description: Deploy a real risk assessor with a specific risk policy configuration. Test it on contract clauses that contain: (1) direct problematic language matching the policy, (2) euphemistic phrasing with equivalent meaning, (3) indirect language that conveys risk through implication, (4) safe language that should not be flagged. Verify that the assessor correctly flags problematic language in all three forms and does not flag safe language. Test with different policy configurations to ensure the assessor respects different risk thresholds and jurisdictional rules.

behaviors.description: The compliance checker correctly determines whether the contract meets all applicable regulatory requirements for the configured jurisdiction.
behaviors.justification: The compliance checker is an AI model that must understand and apply jurisdiction-specific regulatory requirements. Mocking would return hardcoded compliance verdicts, hiding whether the real model actually understands the regulations and applies them correctly to diverse contract structures. Testing requires calling the real compliance checker with contracts that vary in their regulatory compliance to verify it identifies which requirements are met or violated.
behaviors.test_description: Deploy a real compliance checker for specific jurisdictions (e.g., GDPR, California, Texas). Create test contracts that: (1) meet all requirements, (2) violate specific requirements individually, (3) violate multiple requirements simultaneously, (4) have edge cases where requirements are implied vs. explicit. Run the checker on each contract and verify it correctly identifies which specific requirements are satisfied and which are violated. Test with multiple jurisdictions to ensure configuration is respected and the checker doesn't cross-apply requirements incorrectly.

behaviors.description: When two reviewers flag the same clause with conflicting assessments (e.g., extractor says 'standard' but risk assessor says 'high risk'), the merge step preserves both assessments with attribution.
behaviors.justification: This tests the merge logic itself, not the individual reviewers. The merge is a deterministic function that combines reviewer outputs. A test using mock reviewers with synthetic conflicting results is sufficient to verify merge behavior without requiring real LLM calls. The merge logic is independent of reviewer accuracy; what matters is whether it preserves conflicts and attributes them correctly. Testing with realistic conflict patterns at Integration level (mocked reviewers, real merge code) is sufficient to catch failures.
behaviors.test_description: Create mock reviewer results that flag the same clause with different assessments: (1) one reviewer marks a clause as 'standard obligation' while another marks it 'high risk', (2) conflicting interpretations of the same date clause, (3) contradictory compliance assessments for the same requirement. Run the real merge function on these conflicting inputs. Verify that the output report includes all assessments with clear attribution (e.g., 'Extractor: standard, Risk Assessor: high risk'). Test handling of 2-way and 3-way conflicts between reviewers. Verify no assessments are dropped, rewritten, or incorrectly merged.

behaviors.description: The full pipeline — dispatching to all three reviewers, collecting results, merging into a unified report — completes correctly for a real contract without losing any individual reviewer's findings.
behaviors.justification: This tests end-to-end orchestration of multiple agentic steps in sequence: dispatching to three real reviewers in parallel, collecting diverse findings, merging without loss or corruption. To verify that findings from all three reviewers are preserved, you need (1) real contract documents, (2) real reviewer calls (agentic) to ensure diverse findings, (3) full orchestrated pipeline. Mocking any reviewer hides whether the real reviewers' findings are actually preserved. Failure modes like race conditions in parallel collection, partial result loss, or dispatcher bugs only manifest at Workflow level when the full orchestration runs with real async operations.
behaviors.test_description: Create a real contract document complex enough to produce diverse findings across all three domains (extraction, risk, compliance). Call the real clause extractor, risk assessor, and compliance checker on the same contract (in parallel if the pipeline is async). Collect all results and verify: (1) the unified report includes findings from all three reviewers with no omissions, (2) no findings are dropped or corrupted during collection or merge, (3) attribution is preserved (each finding is labeled with its source reviewer), (4) the merge correctly handles any conflicting assessments from different reviewers. Repeat with different contracts of varying complexity and length to ensure robustness. Monitor for race conditions, timeouts, and partial failures in parallel execution.
